channel.title: Edgar's Personal Wiki
channel.description: Last 10 notes on Edgar's Personal Wiki

item.title: athena-ares-metis
item.description: Athena / Ares / Metis In Cryptonomicon, Enoch Root gives Randy Waterhouse an explicit theory of civilization, violence, and technology while they are both in jail in Manila.

item.title: jack-shaftoes-paris-hallucination
item.description: Jack Shaftoe’s Paris Hallucination Jack Shaftoe’s Paris hallucination closes the current ingested portion of Quicksilver by turning physical collapse, theological argument, class humiliation, and quicksilver imagery into one grotesque street pageant.

item.title: logic-mill
item.description: Logic Mill The Logic Mill is Daniel Waterhouse’s unfinished automatic computation project in Quicksilver.

item.title: newton-leibniz-priority-dispute
item.description: Newton-Leibniz Priority Dispute In Quicksilver, the Newton-Leibniz priority dispute is the conflict over who first invented the calculus.

item.title: quicksilver-commerce-and-money
item.description: Quicksilver, Commerce, and Money In Quicksilver, quicksilver is both a material substance and a governing metaphor.

item.title: vagabondage-in-quicksilver
item.description: Vagabondage in Quicksilver In Quicksilver, vagabondage is not only poverty or criminality.

item.title: daniel-waterhouse
item.description: Daniel Waterhouse Fictional natural philosopher in Neal Stephenson’s Quicksilver.

item.title: eliza
item.description: Eliza Fictional Qwghlmian-born former slave, trader, cipher correspondent, and political operator in Neal Stephenson’s Quicksilver.

item.title: enoch-root
item.description: Enoch Root Recurring Neal Stephenson character appearing in both Quicksilver and Cryptonomicon.

item.title: gottfried-wilhelm-leibniz
item.description: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz Historical philosopher, mathematician, jurist, librarian, and court adviser fictionalized in Neal Stephenson’s Quicksilver.
